title: AQA — AI Question Answer | Open Specification for AI-Optimized Q&A
meta_description: AQA is an open specification for enriching FAQ structured data with metadata that AI systems need. Schema.org compatible, MIT licensed, free forever.
og_description: Open specification for structured Q&A content optimized for AI comprehension and citation.
subtitle: Open specification for structured Q&A content optimized for AI comprehension and citation.
problem_text: FAQ pages are everywhere, but Schema.org FAQPage gives AI systems almost nothing to assess answer quality. There are no per-question dates, no changelog, no source citations per answer, and no declared maintenance frequency. An AI crawler cannot tell whether an answer was written yesterday or three years ago, or whether anyone is still maintaining it.
solution_text: AQA is a documented convention that enriches existing Schema.org types (Article, FAQPage, Question, Answer) with a small set of extension properties via a custom JSON-LD context. No new types are invented, no breaking changes are introduced. Validators that do not understand the AQA namespace silently ignore the extra properties. Full backward compatibility is preserved.

signals.desc: Exact freshness of each individual answer
Why and when each answer was modified
What feeds and publications the publisher watches
Granular AI rights: RAG, training, citation, commercial use
Webhook: AI sends missing questions back

basic_desc: Every answer has a known age and at least one source citation. The document declares its language and author.
standard_desc: Content is actively maintained, changes are tracked with a changelog, and industry context is explicit via sector classification and monitoring sources.
full_desc: Full provenance chain: who wrote each answer, what changed, when, why, and from where. Per-question authorship and versioned changelogs.
shield_text: When every question in a document includes both aiUsagePolicy and contentSignature, the document qualifies for AQA Shield. The policy declares permitted AI uses (RAG citation, training, summarization, quoting, commercial use). The signature provides SHA-256 hash verification against content alteration. Shield works at any conformance level as an orthogonal guarantee combining legal protection and cryptographic integrity.
quickstart_migration_desc: step-by-step instructions to add AQA to existing FAQ pages.
distribution_items: AQA Hub Protocol — centralized update aggregation

resources.desc: 8-step guide from bare HTML FAQ to AQA

resources.desc: validate AQA blocks from file or URL (0-100 scoring)

faq_intro: This FAQ implements AQA Full + Shield. View the page source to see the JSON-LD block.

faq.a: AQA (AI Question Answer) is an open specification that enriches Schema.org FAQPage structured data with metadata that AI systems need to assess answer quality, freshness, and provenance. It uses a custom JSON-LD context to add extension properties like per-question dates, citations, changelogs, and AI usage policies to existing Schema.org types. AQA is MIT-licensed, free forever, and has no vendor lock-in.

Yes. AQA uses only existing Schema.org types (Article, FAQPage, Question, Answer) and adds extension properties through a JSON-LD context. Validators that do not understand the AQA namespace silently ignore the extra properties. Existing Schema.org markup continues to work unchanged. AQA is a strict superset — every valid AQA block is also a valid Schema.org document.

faq.q: What are the AQA conformance levels?
faq.a: AQA defines three conformance levels. Basic requires per-question dates, at least one citation per answer, and document-level author and language. Standard adds update frequency, changelog, question versioning, sector classification, and structured citations. Full requires monitoring sources, per-question authorship with credentials, and complete change provenance. All levels are cumulative — Standard includes everything in Basic, and Full includes everything in Standard.

AQA Shield is an orthogonal protection mechanism available at any conformance level. A document qualifies for AQA Shield when every question includes both an aiUsagePolicy (declaring permitted AI uses such as RAG citation, training, summarization, quoting, and commercial use) and a contentSignature (SHA-256 hash proving answer integrity). Shield combines legal declaration with cryptographic verification.

faq.q: How do I implement AQA on my website?
faq.a: Add a JSON-LD script block to your FAQ page with a dual context referencing both Schema.org and the AQA context at https://aqa-spec.org/ns/context.jsonld. Wrap your FAQ in an Article containing a FAQPage, add per-question dateCreated, dateModified, and citation properties, then validate with the Python validator. The migration guide on GitHub provides 8 detailed steps from bare HTML FAQ to full AQA implementation.

philosophy_text_1: AQA is open, free forever, and has no vendor lock-in. Anyone can implement it, validate it, and extend it. The specification is MIT-licensed and community-driven.
philosophy_text_2: The goal is to do for AI visibility what OpenGraph did for social sharing: give publishers a simple, standard way to make their content machine-understandable without breaking anything that already works.
